elements.sent-2411-001: Energy-based fragmentation methods approximate the potential energy of a molecular system as a sum of contribution terms built from the energies of particular subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Some such methods reduce to truncations of the many-body expansion (MBE); others combine subsystem energies in a manner inspired by the principle of inclusion/exclusion (PIE).
elements.sent-2411-003: The combinatorial technique of Möbius inversion of sums over partially ordered sets, which generalizes the PIE, is known to provide a non-recursive expression for the MBE contribution terms, and has also been connected to related cluster expansion methods.
elements.sent-2411-004: We build from these ideas a very general framework for decomposing potential functions into energetic contribution terms associated with elements of particular partially ordered sets (posets) and direct products thereof.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specific choices immediately reproduce not only the MBE, but also a number of other existing decomposition forms, including, e.g., the multilevel ML-BOSSANOVA schema.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, a different choice of poset product leads to a setup familiar from the combination technique for high-dimensional approximation, which has a known connection to quantum-chemical composite methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: We present the ML-SUPANOVA decomposition form, which allows the further refinement of the terms of an MBE-like expansion of the Born-Oppenheimer potential according to systematic hierarchies of ab initio methods and of basis sets.
elements.sent-2411-008: We outline an adaptive algorithm for the a posteori construction of quasi-optimal truncations of this decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-009: Some initial experiments are reported and discussed.
elements.sent-2411-010: The generally high-dimensional nature of the electronic Schrödinger equation renders it inaccessible to conventional numerical techniques.
elements.sent-2411-011: Some recourse is possible to the specialized mechanisms of computational quantum chemistry~\cite{szabo1996, cances2003, echenique2007, helgaker2000, jensen2017 , but the involved costs still scale superlinearly, so these cannot be applied to larger molecular systems, especially not to an acceptable level of accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-012: A common tactic is to decompose the single overarching electronic problem into subproblems.
elements.sent-2411-013: Solutions to these can approximated independently, and combined back into an approximate solution for the original problem.
elements.sent-2411-014: Distinct versions of this tactic are recognisably present in composite methods~\cite{zaspel2019, raghavachari2015, karton2022 , and in energy-based fragmentation methods~\cite{gordon2011, collins2015, raghavachari2015, herbert2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The working formulae for fragmentation methods are often related to or inspired by the well-known many-body expansion (MBE)~\cite{suarez2009, richard2012, chinnamsetty2018, herbert2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Extensions apply counting arguments founded in the principle of inclusion/exclusion (PIE)~\cite{richard2012, richard2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Significant overlap exists here with those fragmentation methods which construct and identify their fragments using ideas from graph theory~\cite{le2012, weiss2010, heber2014, griebel2014, chinnamsetty2018, ricard2020, zhang2021, seeber2023 , and also with multilevel fragmentation methods~\cite{dahlke2007, beran2009, rezac2010, bates2011, chinnamsetty2018, ricard2020, hellmers2021, zhang2021 , which combine the results of subsystem calculations performed at differing levels of theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Much work has gone into understanding the properties of and relationships between different fragmentation methods, often with an eye to their unification~\cite{richard2012, richard2013, richard2014, koenig2016, hellmers2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: We suggest that the combinatorial theory of Möbius inversion~\cite{rota1964, aigner1997, stanley2012 , which can be understood as an extension of inclusion/exclusion, provides a useful theoretical framework, not only for the comparison and analysis of existing fragmentation methods, but also for the development of new ones.}

elements.sent-2411-031: The structure is as follows: In Section~\ref{sec:decomp_techniques , we progressively develop and motivate our new multilevel decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Section~\ref{sec:fundamentals draws on fundamentals in computational chemistry to establish a hierarchy of model approximations to the Born-Oppenheimer potential function, much like those used in composite methods.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Section~\ref{sec:ebfms establishes some basics regarding fragmentation methods.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Section~\ref{sec:inversion introduces the combinatorial tools that we need to build and operate our multilevel scheme, and demonstrates their application.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In Section~\ref{sec:graphs , we use these tools to uncover an issue in the BOSSANOVA setup, and consider the introduction of convex subgraphs as a partial panacea.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Section~\ref{sec:ml combines ideas from both composite methods and multilevel energy-based fragmentation methods, and introduces the final ML-SUPANOVA decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We outline in Section~\ref{sec:adaptive an adaptive a posteori algorithm for generating cost-effective truncations of this decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Some initial experimental results are discussed in Section~\ref{sec:experiments , and we make some concluding remarks in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: A model hierarchy and composite methods
elements.sent-2411-039: For general background on computational quantum chemistry, we refer to~\cite{szabo1996, cances2003, echenique2007, helgaker2000, jensen2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The electronic Schrödinger equation poses a linear partial differential eigenproblem, \begin{equation}\label{eq:schroedinger} H\Psi\pdparen{x_1, \ldots, x_N} = E \Psi\pdparen{x_1, \ldots, x_N}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: Here, $\Psi$ is the electronic wavefunction of a molecular system composed of $M$ clamped nuclei and $N$ electrons, the latter with spatial/spin coordinates $\pdbrace{x_i = \pdparen{r_i \in \bbR^3, \sigma_i \in \pdbrace{\pm{}\sfrac{1 {2 _{i=1 ^N$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The Schrödinger equation --- with ``electronic'' implicit from this point --- is usually cast into atomic units, and energies are measured in Hartrees (\unit{\hartree ).}
elements.sent-2411-043: Solutions to the weak form of~\eqref{eq:schroedinger live in \begin{equation}\mathcal{V} \coloneq H^1\pdparen*{ \pdparen*{ \bbR^3 \times \pdbrace*{\pm\sfrac{1}{2}} }^{N} } \cap \bigwedge_{i=1}^N L^2\pdparen*{\bbR^3 \times \pdbrace*{\pm\sfrac{1}{2}}},\end{equation} see~\cite{schneider2009, cances2003 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: We will refer to as the Born-Oppenheimer potential the function \begin{equation}\label{eq:bo_potential} V^{\text{BO}}\pdparen{X_1, \ldots, X_M} \coloneq \sum_{1 \leq A < B \leq M} \frac{Z_AZ_B}{\norm{R_B - R_A}_2} + \inf_{\substack{\Psi \in \mathcal{V}\\\pdprod{\Psi, \Psi} = 1}} \ \pdprod{\Psi, H\Psi}\end{equation} delivering the ground-state total energy under the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, cf.~\cite{chinnamsetty2018 .}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:bo_potential} V^{BO}\pdparen{X_1, \ldots, X_M} \coloneq \sum_{1 \leq A < B \leq M} \frac{Z_AZ_B}{\norm{R_B - R_A}_2} + \inf_{\substack{\Psi \in V\\\pdprod{\Psi, \Psi} = 1}} \ \pdprod{\Psi, H\Psi}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: This includes the repulsion energy contributed by the nuclei with coordinates $\pdbrace{X_A = (R_A \in \bbR^3, Z_A \in \bbN) _{A=1 ^M$, where each $R_A$ is a spatial coordinate and each $Z_A$ is an atomic number.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Quantum chemical ab initio methods approximate the minimizing $\Psi$ in~\eqref{eq:bo_potential via the Hartree-Fock ansatz~\cite{szabo1996 .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, $\Psi$ is restricted to the space of single Slater determinants, each an antisymmetrized combination of a particular set of $N$ spin orbitals, $\pdbrace{\chi_i : H^1\pdparen{\bbR^3 \times \pdbrace{\pm\sfrac{1 {2 _{i=1 ^N$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The spatial components of these are approximated in a discretization of $H^1\pdparen{\bbR^3}$ via a practically finite basis set of $N_{\text{AO }$ atomic orbital functions $\pdbrace{\phi_{\mu \in H^1\pdparen{\bbR^3 _{\mu=1 ^{N_{\text{AO }$.}}}}}}}

R$ \State $E \gets E - \sum_{\bm{p} \in R} M^{\pdparen{\bm{p}}}$ \label{algline:discount_from_error} \LComment{Calculate approximation, error indicator, cost, and uncertainty} \State $S_i \gets \Call{Reduce}{D \odot L}$ \label{algline:calculate_sum} \State $\mathcal{E}_i \gets \Call{Reduce}{E \odot L}$ \label{algline:calculate_error_indicator} \State $\mathcal{C}_i \gets \sum_{\bm{p} \in I} \mathcal{C}\pdparen{\bm{p}}$ \label{algline:calculate_cumulative_abstract_cost} \State $\text{d}S_i \gets \sqrt{\Call{Reduce}{\varepsilon^2\pdparen{D \odot D}}}$ \label{algline:calculate_propagated_uncertainty} \Until{$Q$ is empty, or termination criteria are met.} \State \Return $I$, and also $S_i$, $\mathcal{E}_i$, $\mathcal{C}_i$, and/or $\text{d}S_i$ as required \end{algorithmic} \caption{Adaptive algorithm for calculation of order ideal $I$ (adapted from~\cite[Alg.~3.3]{barker2024})}\label{alg:adaptive}
